text: Justice is the right ordering of the soul, not just an external rule — the rational, spirited, and appetitive parts each doing what they are meant to do, with reason in command.

The ideal city mirrors the just soul: rulers correspond to reason, guardians to spirit, and producers to appetite. A society is just when each class performs its proper function and does not meddle in another's.

Most people live as prisoners in the cave — mistaking the shadows of opinion for reality. Education is not the pouring of knowledge into a soul, it is the painful turning of the soul towards the light.
Only those who have grasped the Form of the Good are fit to rule, because only they can see what is genuinely best for the city rather than what merely seems best to them.
The Form of the Good is to the world of being what the Sun is to the world of becoming — it is what makes both the objects of knowledge intelligible and the knower able to know.
Mathematics is a ladder out of the visible world: it accustoms the soul to objects that are wholly intelligible, training it to handle the Forms.
Regimes degenerate predictably — aristocracy → timocracy → oligarchy → democracy → tyranny — because each successive regime indulges a lower part of the soul.
Democracy decays into tyranny when freedom becomes license: a population that cannot tolerate any constraint will submit to a single strongman who promises to free them from law itself.
The unjust life is the most miserable, because the tyrant inside the tyrannical soul is enslaved to fear and unbounded desire, even on the throne.
Poetry that merely imitates the visible world is a copy of a copy. Censoring such art in the ideal city is not philistinism, it is the recognition that what we admire shapes who we become.
The philosopher returns to the cave not for self-interest but as a duty to those still bound by shadows — even though they may, like Socrates, kill them for it.
